title: Gen Z Foot Traffic Patterns
verdict: The generation that grew up online shops in person differently, and landlords who understand the difference will win.
evidence: Foot traffic analytics across major U.S. retail corridors show that consumers aged 18 to 27 visit physical stores more frequently than millennials did at the same age, but with markedly different behavior. Gen Z shoppers spend less time per visit, visit more stores per trip, and are disproportionately drawn to environments that emphasize discovery, sensory experience, and social content creation. Conversion rates are highest in retail environments that blend categories, incorporating food, wellness, and cultural programming alongside traditional merchandise. Stores with Instagram-friendly interiors report dwell times 20% longer than comparable formats without visual differentiation.
frame: This generation does not distinguish between shopping and socializing in the way their predecessors did, which means the retail environments they favor look more like curated neighborhoods than traditional malls. For landlords, the takeaway is that tenant mix and adjacency planning matter more than individual tenant credit. A corridor that sequences a vintage shop, a matcha bar, and a piercing studio will outperform three national credit tenants on foot traffic metrics that increasingly drive rent growth. The physical environment itself is the product, and the landlord who programs it well is the one collecting the premium.
